false_positive_detection_workflow.overview: Comprehensive workflow for detecting false positive CVE alerts by analyzing source code to determine if vulnerable package functions are actually used in exploitable ways. This prevents unnecessary package upgrades and potential breaking changes.

step.name: Extract CVE Context from Issue
step.purpose: Understand what the vulnerability is and how it can be exploited

actions.action: Parse GitHub issue for CVE ID, package name, and version
Extract vulnerability type (RCE, XSS, SQLi, etc.)
Identify specific vulnerable functions/methods from CVE description
Note attack vector and exploitation requirements
Record CVSS score and severity level

step.example: CVE-2023-30861 in Flask 2.2.0: - Type: Cookie parsing vulnerability - Vulnerable function: werkzeug.http.parse_cookie() - Attack vector: Malicious cookie headers - Exploitation: Requires user-controlled cookie input

step.name: Search Codebase for Package Usage
step.purpose: Find all locations where the vulnerable package is imported or used

tool.name: search_files
tool.usage: Search for import statements and package references

search_strategy.search: Direct imports: "import flask" or "from flask import"

example.command: search_files with: path: . regex: (import flask|from flask) file_pattern: *.py

step.name: Search for Vulnerable Function Calls
step.purpose: Determine if specific vulnerable functions are actually called

actions.action: Search for vulnerable function names in codebase
Check if vulnerable methods are invoked
Look for vulnerable API endpoints being used

step.example:
For CVE-2023-30861 (Flask cookie parsing):
search_files with: path: . regex: (parse_cookie|request\.cookies|session\[) file_pattern: *.py

step.name: Analyze Usage Context
step.purpose: Understand how the package is used and if vulnerable paths are reachable

analysis_checklist.item: Read files that import the vulnerable package
Trace data flow to vulnerable functions
Check if user input reaches vulnerable code
Evaluate if vulnerable features are enabled

pattern.name: unused_import
transitive_dependency
pattern.description: Package is transitive dependency, never directly imported
pattern.example:
# Application uses requests library import requests
# requests depends on urllib3, which has CVE # But urllib3 is never directly imported or used

pattern.name: vulnerable_function_not_called
pattern.description: Package used but vulnerable function never called

pattern.name: unreachable_code_path
pattern.description: Vulnerable function exists but code path is unreachable

pattern.name: mitigated_by_controls
pattern.description: Vulnerable function used but security controls prevent exploitation

pattern.name: vulnerable_function_called
pattern.description: Vulnerable function is directly called with user input

step.name: Evaluate Deployment Context
step.purpose: Assess if the deployment environment makes exploitation possible

factor.name: exposure
factor.question: Is the service exposed to untrusted users?

factor.name: input_source
factor.question: Where does input to vulnerable function come from?

false_positive_indicators.indicator: Input from trusted internal systems only
Input is hardcoded or from configuration
No user-controlled input reaches vulnerable code

factor.name: security_controls
factor.question: Are there security controls that prevent exploitation?

step.name: Make False Positive Determination
step.purpose: Decide if the CVE is a false positive or true positive

false_positive_criteria.criterion: Package is transitive dependency never directly used
Vulnerable functions are not called anywhere in codebase
Vulnerable code paths are unreachable due to application logic
Service is not exposed to untrusted input

true_positive_criteria.criterion: Vulnerable functions are directly called with user input
Service is exposed to untrusted users
Package is directly imported and used

decision_rules.rule: If ANY critical true positive criterion is met → TRUE POSITIVE
If ALL high priority false positive criteria are met → FALSE POSITIVE
If uncertain or mixed signals → Default to TRUE POSITIVE (safer)
Document reasoning clearly regardless of decision

step.name: Document Analysis
step.purpose: Create comprehensive documentation of the analysis

step.name: Take Action Based on Determination
step.purpose: Execute appropriate workflow based on false positive decision

action_for_false_positive.title: If FALSE POSITIVE

steps.step: Create detailed comment on GitHub issue with analysis
Request they approve devops-approval environment if they agree
Explain that approval triggers Concert API update
Do NOT create branch or PR
Proceed with normal CVE remediation workflow

scenario.name: transitive_dependency
scenario.description: Package is a dependency of a dependency, never directly used
scenario.detection: No direct imports found in codebase
scenario.example: Application uses `requests`, which depends on `urllib3`. CVE in `urllib3` but application never imports or uses `urllib3` directly.

scenario.name: unused_feature
scenario.description: Vulnerable feature of package is not used
scenario.detection: Package imported but vulnerable functions never called
scenario.example: Flask application uses only basic routing, never uses sessions or cookies. CVE affects cookie parsing, but application doesn't use cookies.

scenario.name: internal_only
scenario.description: Service is internal-only, not exposed to untrusted users
scenario.example: Service runs on localhost or behind VPN, requires authentication. Even if vulnerable code exists, not exploitable from external network.

scenario.name: input_validation
scenario.detection: Security controls validate input before vulnerable code
scenario.example: WAF or middleware validates all input before reaching vulnerable function. Malicious input is blocked before it can trigger vulnerability.

scenario.name: disabled_feature
scenario.description: Vulnerable feature is disabled in configuration
scenario.detection: Configuration files show feature is disabled
scenario.example: Vulnerable XML parsing feature is disabled in configuration. Even though package has CVE, feature is not enabled.

best_practices.practice: Always perform false positive analysis before any package changes
Be thorough - check all files, not just obvious ones
Document all findings with specific file paths and line numbers
Include code snippets as evidence in analysis
When uncertain, default to TRUE POSITIVE (safer approach)
Consider deployment context and security controls
Tag appropriate engineer for review of false positive determination

error.description: Cannot definitively determine if false positive
error.action: Default to TRUE POSITIVE and proceed with remediation
error.rationale: Better to upgrade unnecessarily than leave vulnerability

error.action: Document the failure and default to TRUE POSITIVE
error.rationale: Cannot perform analysis without code access

error.description: Codebase too complex to analyze fully
error.action: Perform best-effort analysis, document limitations, default to TRUE POSITIVE if uncertain
error.rationale: Complexity increases risk of missing vulnerable code paths
